meta.title: Maths Through Coding, Learn Math by Building It in Python (Ages 10-15)
meta.description: Live online course where students learn mathematics by programming it: fractions, algebra, geometry, probability and functions built as Python projects. The signature Modern Age Coders fusion, taught by mentors fluent in both. 8 one-hour classes a month, free demo first.
keywords: math and coding course for kids
meta.commitment: 2 live one-hour classes per week + one small build between classes
meta.certification: Course completion certificate + a portfolio of working math programs

program_overview.title: Maths Through Coding
program_overview.tagline: If you can build it, you understand it. Students build the maths.
program_overview.description: A 9-month live course teaching mathematics the way our whole school believes it should be met: by building it. Students write Python programs that ARE the maths, a fraction simplifier that forces understanding of GCD, turtle geometry that makes angles visceral, probability simulators that settle arguments with ten thousand dice rolls, and function grapher projects that turn algebra into something you can watch. This is the signature Modern Age Coders fusion, taught by mentors genuinely fluent in both subjects, and for Ontario families, it happens to cover the coding expectations now examined inside math class from Grade 1. No prior coding needed; the mathematics runs roughly grades 5-9 territory with stretch room.
what_makes_this_different: The maths is the syllabus, the code is the method: every program exists to force a mathematical idea into the open
A computer rejects vague understanding, code that half-understands fractions simply does not run, which makes debugging a mathematics lesson
Mentors fluent in both subjects, because a coding teacher who waves at the maths (or vice versa) breaks the whole idea
Real Python from week one, at a pace built for ages 10-15
Every topic ends with a working artifact the student built and can demo
Directly serves Ontario's coding-in-math strand, and the computational-thinking push in curricula worldwide
The engagement answer for the child who says maths is boring but plays with code for hours, or the reverse
One dedicated mentor, one full interactive hour, twice a week

learning_path.phase_1: Numbers as programs: Python foundations through arithmetic, divisibility, fractions and number patterns
learning_path.phase_2: Shape and change: turtle geometry, coordinates, and algebra brought alive through variables, functions and graphing
learning_path.phase_3: Chance and data: probability simulators, statistics on real datasets, and a capstone project of the student's own design

career_outcomes: Mathematics that survives, because it was built rather than memorized
Real Python skills, the on-ramp to our full programming tracks
Computational thinking: decomposition, patterns and precision, the literacy every future field wants
A demo-able project portfolio that makes school maths and science genuinely easier

phase_1_foundation.title: PHASE 1: Numbers as Programs (Months 1-3)
phase_1_foundation.description: Python foundations learned through number theory a student can touch: parity, divisibility, primes, factors and fractions. By phase end the student programs comfortably and, the real goal, has met arithmetic ideas so concretely they can never again be mysterious.

month_1.title: Month 1: First Programs, First Number Ideas

week_1.title: Hello Python: Editor, Print and Variables
week_1.description: Hello, Python: the editor, print, variables, and a diagnostic of both maths comfort and machine comfort
topics: Setting up and using the Python editor
week_1.practice: Write and run a short program that stores your name and age in variables and prints a sentence.
projects: Write a Python script that prints a personalised greeting built from variables

week_2.title: Arithmetic Operators and Order of Operations
week_2.description: Arithmetic operators and order of operations: the computer obeys the same grammar your maths teacher does
topics: Addition, subtraction, multiplication and division operators
Matching code grammar to maths grammar
Predicting an expression before running it
week_2.practice: Predict the value of several mixed-operator expressions, then check each by running it.
projects: Write a Python calculator that evaluates a multi-step expression and prints the result

week_3.title: Integer Division, Remainders and Modulo
week_3.description: Integer division and remainders: modulo as the parity machine, even/odd detectors and cycle patterns
topics: Cycle and repeating patterns with modulo
week_3.practice: Use modulo to test a list of numbers for even or odd and describe the pattern you see.
projects: Write a Python script that labels every number from 1 to 50 as even or odd using modulo

week_4.title: Conditionals and Divisibility Rules
week_4.description: Conditionals with number tests: divisibility rules coded and then explained backwards
week_4.practice: Code a divisibility checker for 2, 3 and 5 and explain in your own words why each rule works.
projects: Write a Python program that reports which of 2, 3, 5 and 10 a number is divisible by

week_5_6.title: Loops and Times-Table Patterns
week_5_6.description: Loops as repetition with purpose: times tables generated, then patterns in them hunted and explained
week_5_6.practice: Generate several times tables with a loop and write down one pattern you notice in each.
projects: Write a Python script that visualises a times-table as a grid of rows and columns

week_7_8.title: Factor Finders and Prime Testers
week_7_8.description: Factor finders and prime testers: the student's first real algorithms, and why 1 is not prime, settled by code
topics: Finding all factors of a number
Why 1 is not prime, settled by code
Testing an algorithm across many inputs
week_7_8.practice: Run your prime tester across the numbers 1 to 30 and confirm the results by hand.
projects: Write a Python program that lists every factor of a number
Write a Python prime tester that prints all primes up to 100

week_9_10.title: GCD and the Fraction Simplifier
week_9_10.description: GCD discovered through the Euclidean idea; a fraction simplifier built, which forces equivalence to be truly understood
topics: The Euclidean idea for finding a GCD
Why the machine forces real understanding
week_9_10.practice: Simplify several fractions by hand, then confirm each one with your simplifier.
projects: Write a Python fraction simplifier that reduces any fraction to lowest terms using the GCD

week_11_12.title: Fraction Arithmetic and Phase Demo Day
week_11_12.description: Fraction arithmetic programmed (common denominators become obvious when a machine needs them); phase demo day: each student presents their number toolkit
topics: Adding and subtracting fractions in code
Why a machine needs common denominators
Combining the GCD with fraction arithmetic
week_11_12.practice: Extend your fraction program to add and subtract two fractions and simplify the result.
week_11_12.assessment: Phase demo day: each student presents their number toolkit and explains the mathematics behind it.
projects: Write a Python fraction calculator that adds, subtracts and simplifies two fractions

phase_2_shape_and_algebra.title: PHASE 2: Shape and Change (Months 4-6)
phase_2_shape_and_algebra.description: Geometry becomes visceral through turtle graphics, exterior angles are unforgettable once you have steered them, and algebra comes alive as code: variables that actually vary, functions as machines, and graphs the student plots themselves.

week_13_14.title: Turtle Basics and the Exterior-Angle Discovery
week_13_14.description: Turtle basics: squares, triangles and the exterior-angle discovery (why 360 divided by n draws every regular polygon)
topics: Why 360 divided by n draws a regular polygon
week_13_14.practice: Draw three different regular polygons with turtle and record the turn angle each one needs.
projects: Write a Python turtle program that draws any regular polygon from the number of sides

week_15_16.title: Star Polygons, Spirals and Angle Art
week_15_16.description: Star polygons, spirals and angle art; interior-angle sums verified by walking them
topics: Spirals with a changing step or angle
Verifying angle sums by walking them
week_15_16.practice: Create an angle-art pattern and explain the interior or exterior angles that make it close.
projects: Write a Python turtle program that draws a star polygon or spiral of your own design

week_17_18.title: The Coordinate Plane in Code
week_17_18.description: The coordinate plane as the screen it literally is; plotting points, lines and midpoints in code
topics: The screen as a coordinate plane
Reading coordinates back off a drawing
week_17_18.practice: Plot a set of points in code and compute the midpoint of two of them.
projects: Write a Python program that plots points and draws the line and midpoint between two of them

week_19_20.title: Algebra as Code: Expressions and Equations
week_19_20.description: Algebra as code: expressions evaluated, equations solved by guess-check-refine, and why balance methods beat brute force
topics: Solving equations by guess, check and refine
Why balance methods beat brute force
week_19_20.practice: Solve a linear equation both with a guess-check-refine loop and by balancing, then compare.
projects: Write a Python script that solves a linear equation by guess-check-refine and prints each step

week_21_22.title: Functions as Machines: Input, Rule, Output
week_21_22.description: Python functions and mathematical functions introduced as the same idea: input, rule, output
topics: Input, rule and output as one idea
Evaluating a function for many inputs
week_21_22.practice: Write a function for a simple rule and print a table of its outputs for several inputs.
projects: Write a Python function that takes x and returns a rule such as 2x plus 1, then tabulates it

week_23_24.title: The Grapher Project and Phase Demo Day
week_23_24.description: The grapher project: plot y = mx + c and watch m and c do their jobs; then parabolas, and phase demo day
topics: The role of the gradient m
The role of the intercept c
Presenting the grapher at demo day
week_23_24.practice: Use your grapher to compare two lines with different m and c and describe the difference.
week_23_24.assessment: Phase demo day: each student presents their grapher and explains how m and c shape the line.
projects: Write a Python grapher that plots y = mx + c for chosen values of m and c
Extend the grapher to plot a parabola and watch how its shape changes

phase_3_chance_data_capstone.title: PHASE 3: Chance, Data and the Capstone (Months 7-9)
phase_3_chance_data_capstone.description: Probability stops being formulas when you can roll ten thousand dice per second: simulators settle intuitions, real datasets get summarized and questioned, and the course closes with a capstone the student designs, a working program where the mathematics is the star.

week_25_26.title: Randomness: Coin Flippers and Dice Rollers
week_25_26.description: Randomness in code: coin flippers and dice rollers; experimental versus theoretical probability, watched converging
topics: Watching results converge as trials grow
week_25_26.practice: Simulate 1000 coin flips and compare the experimental probability with the theoretical value.
projects: Write a Python script that simulates thousands of dice rolls and reports the proportion of each outcome

week_27_28.title: Simulating Classic Probability Puzzles
week_27_28.description: The classic puzzles simulated: two-dice sums, streaks in coin flips, intuition calibrated by evidence
topics: Why 7 is the most likely sum
week_27_28.practice: Simulate two-dice sums many times and chart how often each sum appears.
projects: Write a Python simulation of two-dice sums that displays the frequency of each total

week_29_30.title: Mean, Median and Range in Code
week_29_30.description: Mean, median and range computed by the student's own code, the definitions can no longer be vague
topics: Checking results against a hand calculation
week_29_30.practice: Write code to compute the mean, median and range of a data list, then verify each by hand.
projects: Write a Python program that reads a list of numbers and reports the mean, median and range

week_31_32.title: Exploring a Real Dataset
week_31_32.description: A real dataset explored: summaries, simple charts, and the sceptic's questions (outliers, sample size, fair comparisons)
topics: Judging whether a comparison is fair
week_31_32.practice: Summarise a small real dataset and write down two sceptic's questions it raises.
projects: Write a Python program that summarises a real dataset and draws a simple chart from it

week_33_34.title: Capstone Chosen and Scoped
week_33_34.description: Capstone chosen and scoped: a times-table trainer, geometry art generator, probability game, data story, the student's call, the mentor's rigor
topics: Options: a trainer, an art generator, a game or a data story
Mentor rigour applied to the maths
week_33_34.practice: Write a one-page plan for your capstone that names the mathematics it will make visible.
projects: Begin building your chosen capstone program in Python from your scoped plan

week_35_36.title: Capstone Build, Test and Demo Day
week_35_36.description: Build, test, polish; demo day, the student presents their program and, more importantly, explains its mathematics
week_35_36.practice: Test your capstone on several cases, fix what breaks, and rehearse your demo explanation.
week_35_36.assessment: Final demo day: each student presents the capstone and explains the mathematics it is built on.
projects: Complete, test and polish your capstone program ready to demo

faqs.question: Is this a maths course or a coding course?
faqs.answer: A maths course that uses code as its laboratory. The syllabus is mathematical, number theory, fractions, geometry, algebra, probability, statistics, and every program exists to force one of those ideas into the open. Students do finish with real Python skills, which is a genuine bonus, but the mathematics is the star.

faqs.question: Does my child need coding experience?
faqs.answer: None. Python is taught from the first hello, at a pace built for ages 10-15. Children who already code a little move faster into the mathematical meat; the mentor adjusts either way.

faqs.question: Why does building things teach maths better than practicing problems?
faqs.answer: Because a computer rejects vague understanding. A student who 'sort of' knows fraction equivalence writes a simplifier that fails, and fixing it requires actually understanding GCD. That debugging loop, predict, test, explain the gap, is the deepest mathematical practice there is, and it feels like play.

faqs.question: We're in Ontario and coding is now examined inside math class. Does this help?
faqs.answer: Directly, this course is our sweet spot for exactly that curriculum. Ontario weaves coding expectations through math from Grade 1, most homes have nobody fluent in both halves, and our mentors teach both natively. Students walk back into class ahead on the strand instead of puzzled by it.

faqs.question: My child loves games but says maths is boring. Will this work?
faqs.answer: This course exists for that child. The maths arrives wearing a project the child wants to build, angle art, a dice game, a grapher, and engagement does the heavy lifting. The reverse child (loves maths, indifferent to screens) discovers code as the best maths toy ever made.

faqs.question: What maths level does it assume and reach?
faqs.answer: It starts from comfortable whole-number arithmetic (roughly grade 5) and reaches into linear functions, probability and statistics (roughly grade 9 territory), with stretch room for strong students. It runs happily alongside any school curriculum and strengthens it rather than clashing.

faqs.question: What does the course cost?
faqs.answer: Small-group classes are ₹1,499 per month and private 1-on-1 mentorship is ₹4,999 per month in India; international students pay USD $100 per month for group and $150 for 1-on-1. Both include 8 live one-hour classes a month, recordings, and a free demo class before any payment.

faqs.question: Can we try it before paying?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every student starts with a free live demo class, they will build and run their first small program inside that hour, and you will see the method working with your own child. No card details required.

testimonials.name: Shewta Singh, mother of Ishan (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My son struggled with math for years. Integrating it into coding projects has transformed his understanding and confidence. Highly recommended!

testimonials.name: Sonam Oswal, mother of Dhairya (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My child Dhairya is really enjoying the classes. This is his first online class, and he eagerly looks forward to it. I can see his improvement.

projects_throughout_course: Number toolkit: parity detector, divisibility tester, prime finder, factor lister
The fraction machine: simplifier and calculator built on real GCD understanding
Turtle geometry gallery: polygons, stars and angle art with the maths explained
Function grapher: lines and parabolas plotted, parameters explored live
Probability simulators and a real-data statistics story
The capstone: a self-designed program where the mathematics is the star
skills_mastered: Number theory made concrete: primes, factors, GCD, modulo
Geometry through construction: angles, polygons, coordinates
Algebra as living code: variables, expressions, functions
Probability by simulation and statistics on real data
Python fundamentals: variables, conditionals, loops, functions

weekly_structure.between_class: One small build or extension challenge
weekly_structure.demo_days: Each phase ends with the student presenting their work

support_provided.live_sessions: 8 live one-hour classes every month, 2 per week
support_provided.mentorship: One mentor fluent in both mathematics and programming
support_provided.progress_tracking: Project portfolio + concept checks shared with parents
support_provided.flexibility: Slots move around school and family plans

certification.final_certificate: Course completion certificate from Modern Age Coders
certification.portfolio_projects: A demo-ready portfolio of 12+ working math programs
certification.pathway: Direct on-ramp to our full Python and AI programming tracks

prerequisites.math_background: Comfortable whole-number arithmetic; everything else is built inside the course
prerequisites.coding_experience: None required, Python taught from zero
prerequisites.equipment: Computer with stable internet; free tools only (no purchases)
prerequisites.motivation: Curiosity about either half, maths or code, is enough; the course supplies the other

who_is_this_for.bored_by_worksheets: The child who says maths is boring but builds in Minecraft for hours
who_is_this_for.maths_lovers: The child who loves maths and deserves the best maths toy ever made
who_is_this_for.ontario_families: Families facing coding-inside-math curriculum expectations with nobody home fluent in both
who_is_this_for.understanding_seekers: Students who pass tests but want (or need) the ideas to actually make sense
who_is_this_for.future_builders: Kids headed for our programming tracks who want the mathematical spine first

career_paths_after_completion: Mathematics that holds through high school, because it was built
Ready for our full Python and AI programming masterclasses
Computational-thinking maturity that shows up in science and school projects
A natural bridge to competition mathematics for the puzzle-hungry

course_guarantees.live_classes: Live, interactive classes with a real instructor, never pre-recorded videos.
course_guarantees.small_batches: Small batches only: group classes are capped at 10 students, with mini-batch (3 to 4 students) and personal 1-on-1 options.
course_guarantees.structured_curriculum: A structured, well-paced curriculum taught step by step, with hands-on practice in every session.
course_guarantees.doubt_support: Doubt support between classes over WhatsApp, so you are never left stuck.
course_guarantees.certificate: A course-completion certificate you can share.
course_guarantees.free_demo: A free demo class before you enrol, so you can decide with no pressure.
